channel.title: Trajectory — News
channel.description: Daily-ish notes on longevity research, FDA actions, and mechanism explainers.

item.title: What I'd actually take if I were 50
item.description: A common question we get: "You have 85 things in the catalogue. What would you actually do?" The honest answer is it depends entirely on who you are. But "it depends" isn't useful, and the wizard-driven personalisation is one approach but not the only one. Sometimes seeing somebody else's reasoning

item.title: Why we have 73 interventions, not 700
item.description: If you Google "longevity supplements," you'll find lists of 200, 500, sometimes 1,000+ compounds. Most of them are theatre. Trajectory has 73 entries. That's not because we're lazy — it's because most things people sell as "longevity interventions" don't have evidence that survives a hostile read. H

item.title: FDA expands tirzepatide label to include cardiovascular event reduction
item.description: Tirzepatide joins semaglutide on the short list of weight-loss drugs with a formal cardiovascular indication — meaningfully changing the insurance and prescribing math.

item.title: Why VO2max is the vital sign nobody measures (and how to measure yours)
item.description: Cardiorespiratory fitness predicts all-cause mortality more strongly than smoking, hypertension, and diabetes — and unlike those, your doctor probably hasn't measured it.

item.title: Stop chasing rapamycin until you're sleeping 7.5 hours
item.description: The rapamycin discourse has gotten ahead of the data. The math says: fix sleep, train VO2max, eat Mediterranean — those have a 50–100× larger expected mortality benefit than any current Frontier intervention.

item.title: New trial readout — creatine and cognitive performance in older adults
item.description: A new 24-week RCT in adults 60+ shows modest but real improvements in working memory and processing speed with 5g/day creatine. Effect was concentrated in vegetarians and sleep-deprived participants — consistent with the dietary-baseline hypothesis.

item.title: The fiber protocol nobody runs — and why your gut ages faster without it
item.description: Most adults eat half the fiber the data supports. Fixing that is one of the highest-leverage, lowest-cost moves available — and it works through three independent mechanisms.

item.title: Zone 2 cardio: what it actually is, and why it isn't a fad
item.description: Zone 2 isn't a heart-rate gimmick — it's the specific intensity at which mitochondria are forced to adapt. Three sessions a week is the dose.

item.title: The protein RDA is wrong for adults over 50
item.description: The 0.8 g/kg/day RDA was set in 1968 for healthy young adults — and it's wrong for almost everyone over 50. The newer evidence-based target is 1.2–1.6 g/kg/day, ideally spread across meals.

item.title: Vitamin D after VITAL: where the evidence actually lands
item.description: VITAL didn't kill vitamin D — it killed the idea that supplementing vitamin D in vitamin-D-replete adults would prevent cancer or cardiovascular events. The fracture prevention case in deficient older adults is still real.

item.title: Grip strength is a better mortality predictor than your blood pressure
item.description: A $20 hand dynamometer measures something more predictive of all-cause mortality than systolic BP, ApoB, or HbA1c. Most adults have never had it tested.
